text: This is an IMAS educational video of robotic cholecystectomy. Robotic cholecystectomy is done with the help of Da Vinci SI high definition surgical system, commonly known as a surgical robot. The surgical robot consists of three main parts. First one is the master console on which the surgeon sits and performs the surgery. The second one is the patient cart which contains the robotic arms which are docked to the ports placed in the patient's body and the instruments are carried by the robotic arms with a sterile interface made by the camera arm drapes and the instrument arm drapes. The third component is the vision card in which the assistants can see whatsoever surgery is going on inside the body with the help of a two-dimensional screen.

The robotic cholecystectomy setup involves inserting a robotic camera port 2 cm above the umbilicus, aligning it with the gallbladder in the right hypochondrium. The patient cart approaches from the right shoulder, while the vision cart is positioned towards the left shoulder. Robotic arm one is placed in the left hypochondrium below the left costal margin using an 8 mm Da Vinci port with a blunt obturator. Robotic arm two is positioned in the right umbilical region or right flank, maintaining at least an 8 cm distance from the camera port to prevent arm clashing. A 5 mm trocar is used for the assistant port in the right flank. After creating pneumoperitoneum and inserting the camera port, the robotic cart is docked, and the surgeon operates from the master console.

The gallbladder fundus is retracted towards the right diaphragm using the assistant port. The surgeon utilizes a robotic hook in arm one and fenestrated bipolar forceps in arm two to perform dissection. The hook acts as a versatile instrument, enabling anterior and posterior dissection, creating a window behind the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved, revealing the cystic artery, cystic duct, and liver surface. Hem-o-lok clips are applied to the duct and artery for division, aided by the three-dimensional high-definition stereoscopic vision.

The surgeon uses routine laparoscopic scissors to divide the cystic duct and artery. The gallbladder is then dissected off and placed into an endobag. The endobag is retrieved through the umbilical trocar after the robot is undocked, and a 5 mm scope is inserted through the left hypochondrial trocar for visualization.
